ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for LaLa Prawn Noodle. FOODON_03302050 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for prawn-clam noodle soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to LaLa Prawn Noodle. No suitable parent code identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of LaLa Prawn Noodle weighs approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, prawns, lala clams, vegetables, and prawn broth.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Noodle Soup (Har Mee) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for prawn noodle soup
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Prawn Noodle Soup (Har Mee) as the closest approved match. LaLa Prawn Noodle is a variant that adds lala clams to the standard prawn noodle base. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Prawn Noodle Soup data (per 100 g cooked, as served) with minor upward adjustment to protein and sodium to account for clam content, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central shellfish and noodle soup entries. Values represent a reasonable estimate for the as-served dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Yellow Noodles and Rice Vermicelli — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Am J Clin Nutr
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for LaLa Prawn Noodle. GI estimated at 52 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for yellow wheat noodles (GI ~50–55, medium-low) and rice vermicelli (GI ~58), weighted by typical noodle composition in a prawn noodle dish (approximately 60% yellow noodles, 40% rice vermicelli). The protein and fat content of the broth, prawns, and clams further attenuate the glycemic response. A composite GI of 52 (low category) is defensible. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (52 × 47.25 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 24.6, rounded to 24.8 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from prawn broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of LaLa Prawn Noodle due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1890 mg per bowl), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg limit; requesting reduced broth volume or consuming less soup is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from noodle carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl of LaLa Prawn Noodle carries a moderate glycemic load (~24.8 per serving); portion control of the noodle component, choosing rice vermicelli over yellow noodles where possible, and pairing with the protein-rich prawn and clam content can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol from prawns and clams
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake of LaLa Prawn Noodle given its elevated sodium content and moderate dietary cholesterol (~55 mg per 100 g from crustaceans and molluscs); however, the dish also provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids from seafood, which may be partially cardioprotective when consumed in controlled portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: LaLa Prawn Noodle (啦啦虾面) is a Singaporean and Malaysian seafood noodle dish that combines the classic prawn noodle soup (har mee) base with the addition of lala clams (short-neck clams, Paphia undulata or similar species). The dish is served in a rich, deeply flavoured prawn-shell broth that is simmered for several hours to extract umami and sweetness from the prawn heads and shells. Yellow wheat noodles and/or rice vermicelli are blanched and placed in the bowl, topped with fresh prawns, steamed or stir-fried lala clams, blanched bean sprouts, and leafy greens such as kangkong (water spinach). The dish is typically garnished with fried shallots, sliced red chilli, and a side of sambal belacan for heat. Some hawker stalls add pork lard for additional richness, making the dish non-halal in those preparations. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, briny, and slightly sweet from the seafood, with a spicy kick from the sambal. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~88 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from prawns and clams (~6.2 g per 100 g), and a moderate carbohydrate load from the noodles (~10.5 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is sodium, as the prawn broth is heavily seasoned and a full bowl can deliver close to 1900 mg of sodium. The dish has a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~52) due to the protein and fat content moderating carbohydrate absorption. LaLa Prawn Noodle is a popular hawker dish found across Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia, particularly in Penang-style and Singapore-style prawn noodle stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lala prawn mee?
What are lala clams in prawn noodle?
Sodium content of prawn noodle soup
Difference between har mee and lala prawn noodle

ingredient_synonyms.lala: short-neck clams, baby clams, Paphia undulata, Venus clams
ingredient_synonyms.har mee: prawn noodle, prawn mee, shrimp noodle soup
ingredient_synonyms.yellow noodles: Hokkien noodles, egg noodles, wheat noodles
ingredient_synonyms.bee hoon: rice vermicelli, rice noodles, mee hoon
ingredient_synonyms.kangkong: water spinach, morning glory, swamp cabbage
ingredient_synonyms.sambal belacan: shrimp paste chilli, sambal, chilli paste

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists specifically for LaLa Prawn Noodle with lala clams. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo Prawn Noodle Soup (Har Mee) entry, which is the closest approved primary source match, with adjustments for clam content cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for the noodle components. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Prawn Noodle Soup data and adjusted for lala clam content; an exact HPB entry for LaLa Prawn Noodle was not identified at time of processing. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in the source data for this seafood noodle dish — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central for comparable dishes. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (52, low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for yellow noodles and rice vermicelli; no direct GI measurement for LaLa Prawn Noodle was found in the approved GI literature. 7. Halal status is uncertain as some hawker preparations include pork lard; is_halal is set to false as a conservative default. 8. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a standard hawker bowl; actual serving sizes may vary between 400–550 g depending on the stall.
